title: Information Deletion — S3 Object Delete Storm Outside Retention Workflow

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.8.10 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.8.10 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.33: Information Deletion — S3 Object Delete Storm Outside Retention Workflow.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.33' running on aws:cloudtrail eventName=DeleteObject, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=aws sourcetype=aws:cloudtrail eventName=DeleteObject earliest=-24h
| stats count as deletes by userIdentity.arn, bucketName
| where deletes>1000
| lookup approved_bulk_delete.csv userIdentity.arn, bucketName OUTPUT approved
| where isnull(approved)
description: Detects mass delete operations not tagged with approved legal-hold workflow ID for controlled deletion.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes aws and sourcetypes aws:cloudtrail. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Tag lifecycle jobs in lookup; (2) correlate with backup jobs; (3) insider risk review. Indexes required: `index=aws`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=aws:cloudtrail`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for the Change data model (Change.All_Changes).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **SIEM maintenance and upgrades** — Monitoring gaps during planned SIEM upgrades, parser updates, or search head restarts documented in the maintenance schedule.

2. **Data source onboarding baseline** — New data sources generating unfamiliar patterns during the initial baselining period before detection rules are tuned.

3. **Alert rule tuning periods** — Elevated false positive rates during detection rule refinement cycles that are part of the continuous improvement process.

4. **Analyst capacity constraints** — Queue depth increases during known capacity gaps (training, conferences) with documented risk acceptance for the period.

5. **Automated triage resolution** — Events resolved by SOAR playbooks without human queue entry are properly handled but not visible in manual review metrics.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`iso27001_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by control owner, risk acceptance reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries at each ISMS management review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase app ID 1876) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for AWS" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `aws` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `A.8.10`. The control objective is: "Evidence that ISO 27001 A.8.10 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.33: Information Deletion — S3 Object Delete Storm Outside Retention Workflow." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for AWS (Splunkbase app ID 1876), landing events in `aws` under `aws:cloudtrail`. CloudTrail management events average 5000-50000 API calls/account/hour for active workloads (~5-50 GB/account/month). Data events (S3, Lambda) can multiply this 10-100x.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > CloudTrail (SQS-based ingest is strongly preferred over S3 polling). A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[aws_cloudtrail://prod_account]
aws_account = production
aws_region = us-east-1
sqs_queue = splunk-ct-queue
index = aws
sourcetype = aws:cloudtrail
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `eventName, eventSource, userIdentity.arn, sourceIPAddress, requestParameters, responseElements, errorCode, userAgent`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=aws earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Tag lifecycle jobs in lookup. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** correlate with backup jobs. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** insider risk review. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

The detection fires when `deletes > 1000`. For "Information Deletion — S3 Object Delete Storm Outside Retention Workflow," the aggregation identifies records at risk of crossing a retention boundary — the control is about time-bound lifecycle enforcement, and the aggregate captures the current state of that lifecycle. This threshold is the operational heart of the control — set it too low and the signal drowns in noise; set it too high and real compliance deviations go undetected. The value `1000` should never be treated as universal; it must be calibrated against your specific operational baseline.

Calibration procedure: before enabling this as a production alert, run the base search over the last 30 days with the threshold removed and compute distribution statistics:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats count by deletes
| stats avg(deletes) as mean, perc50(deletes) as p50, perc90(deletes) as p90, perc99(deletes) as p99, max(deletes) as hi
```

Set the production threshold between p90 and p99 depending on your organisation's alert fatigue tolerance. Document the chosen value in the control specification alongside the date of calibration and the sample size — auditors will ask how you arrived at `1000` and the defensible answer is 'data, plus a documented review by the control owner,' not 'the template said so.'

The `count()` aggregation over the last 24 hours is a deliberate choice. Compliance reports on sustained behaviour, not instantaneous spikes — a single noisy event crossing the threshold for one second should not page the on-call. If your organisation requires point-in-time sensitivity (e.g. real-time fraud detection), shorten the time window and aggregation span; for accountability reporting, widen them. Tune for the regulation's actual expectation, not engineering preference.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Change` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Change.All_Changes by All_Changes.action, All_Changes.object_category, All_Changes.user | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Change` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.6.33: Information Deletion — S3 Object Delete Storm Outside Retent]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.6.33,reg=ISO_27001
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=aws sourcetype=aws:cloudtrail earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `bucketName`, `deletes`, `userIdentity.arn`. Run:

```spl
index=aws sourcetype=aws:cloudtrail earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table bucketName, deletes, userIdentity.arn
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(d) Threshold calibration:** Before enabling as a production alert, run the base search with the threshold removed and profile the distribution of `deletes`:

```spl
<base search> earliest=-30d
| stats perc50(deletes) as p50 perc90(deletes) as p90 perc99(deletes) as p99 max(deletes) as hi
```

The current threshold `1000` should sit between p90 and p99 for a balanced signal-to-noise ratio. If it's below p50, expect heavy alert volume; if above p99, expect under-detection.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.6.33: ISO 27001 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (the key fields):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Time trend (the key fields):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Too many or too few alerts:** The threshold is almost certainly miscalibrated for your estate — re-run the percentile calibration (Step 3d) on a representative 30-day window and pick a value between p90 and p99.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**ISO 27001-specific failure modes:**

• Statement of Applicability (SoA) drift: ISO 27001 requires a SoA listing which Annex A controls are applicable and which are excluded with justification. A detection for a control excluded in the SoA adds noise to the audit evidence pile; reconcile UC-to-SoA mapping annually. Maintain `iso27001_soa.csv` with current applicability.

• Context of the organisation (Cl. 4): controls calibrated for a 50-person company's threat model do not suit a 5000-person multinational's. Detection thresholds, log retention, and escalation paths must align with the organisation's ISMS scope — a single 'enterprise default' for ISO 27001 is a tell-tale sign of a cookie-cutter implementation.

• Internal audit programme (Cl. 9.2) evidence: detections that support internal audits must produce records that can be attached to audit evidence packs. Tag the alert payload with audit_cycle=YYYY-HN so evidence bundling is trivial.

• Management review inputs (Cl. 9.3): control effectiveness metrics must be reviewable at management review. A detection that only raises alerts (no trend metric) cannot be brought to the management meeting; schedule a companion report that produces trend data per control.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Information Deletion — S3 Object Delete Storm Outside Retention Workflow. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Disable a test log forwarder on a security-relevant host; verify the monitoring search detects the forwarding gap within 30 minutes and generates an alert with hostname, expected data, and last-received timestamp.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Confirm all test log forwarders are active and sending data within expected intervals; verify no forwarding gap alert fires and the monitoring dashboard shows all sources as healthy.

evidence: Monitoring coverage report showing data sources, detection rules, alert volumes, investigation outcomes, and effectiveness metrics for ISMS management review.
exclusions: SIEM planned maintenance windows; new data source baselining periods; automated triage closures; analyst capacity gaps during documented training periods; detection rule tuning windows.
